summary: Martin Fowler explains his personal approach to using Twitter effectively, emphasizing curation over engagement. He values Twitter's brevity constraint and uses it primarily to discover interesting articles rather than for conversations, having learned in the 90s that online discussions rarely prove worthwhile. He carefully curates who he follows, uses lists to organize topic-specific feeds, insists on chronological ordering, and avoids replies and arguments. He acknowledges Twitter's harassment problems while noting his privileged position in not being targeted, and expresses skepticism about Musk's understanding of content moderation challenges.
key_points: Twitter's character limit is a feature, not a bug - it forces brevity and makes skimming efficient
Online conversations have been toxic since the 90s newsgroups; Twitter just continues the pattern
Careful curation of who you follow is essential - some prolific accounts are best experienced through retweets
Lists are underutilized but powerful for topic-based reading at appropriate times
Having your own internet domain provides independence from platform instability
money_quotes: One of the big lessons I learned about the internet in the 90's was that conversations online were rarely worthwhile.
90's newsgroups had all the toxic behavior we see on online platforms now, for much the same reasons.

It's easy for me to underweight the importance of a problem I don't have to deal with, but I know good people who avoid online spaces entirely because of a fear of harassment, often a fear born of bitter experience.
Some people tweet a lot of good stuff, but too much - those I don't follow reckoning that anything particularly good will be retweeted by those that I do follow.
I try to avoid getting into arguments, and am mostly successful at that.
As ever, I'm happy that I have my own internet domain.
key_insight: Twitter becomes useful when you treat it as a curated discovery feed rather than a conversation platform, deliberately avoiding the engagement traps that make social media toxic.
